curriculum_source: CFA Program 2027 Level III, Volume 5 (Ethical and Professional Standards), Learning Module 2: Guidance for Standard I: Professionalism

drills.los_text: demonstrate a thorough knowledge of the CFA Institute Code of Ethics and Standards of Professional Conduct by interpreting the Code and Standards in various situations involving issues of professional integrity

questions.stem: *Multi-jurisdiction scenario: no applicable local law* — MNPI use *most likely*:

options.A: must adhere to Code and Standards.
options.B: may act on MNPI where no local law prohibits.
options.C: may act if local exchange is silent.

rationales.A:
Full statement: must adhere to Code and Standards; where no applicable law exists, must adhere to Code and Standards, which prohibit use of material nonpublic information.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 1 Solution: 'in the absence of specific applicable law or other regulatory requirements, the Code and Standards govern the member's or candidate's actions... when no applicable law exists, members and candidates are required to adhere to the Code and Standards, and the Code and Standards prohibit the use of material nonpublic information.'
rationales.B: Standards fill absence of law.
rationales.C: Silence does not permit.

questions.stem: *Exhibit 1 NS-resides / LS-does business* *most likely* member must adhere to:

options.A: The member has no duty beyond LS-country law because that is where business is conducted.
options.B: Code and Standards; because applicable law (LS) is less strict than the Code and Standards, member must adhere to the Code and Standards.
options.C: The member must follow LS-country law, since applicable local law always governs over the Code.

rationales.A: Incorrect. When applicable law is less strict than the Code and Standards, the member must adhere to the Code and Standards.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Exhibit 1 (Application of the Code and Standards): 'Member resides in NS country, does business in LS country; LS law applies. Duties: Member must adhere to the Code and Standards. Explanation: Because applicable law is less strict than the Code and Standards, the member must adhere to the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The Code and Standards apply because they are stricter than the applicable (LS) law.

questions.stem: *Exhibit 1 LS-resides / MS-does business, MS applies* *most likely* member adheres to:

options.A: The member should follow the Code and Standards because they supersede any national law.
options.B: The member should follow LS-country law because that is where the member resides.
options.C: MS country law; because applicable law (MS) is stricter than Code and Standards, member must adhere to the stricter applicable law.

rationales.A: Incorrect. When applicable law (MS) is STRICTER than the Code and Standards, the stricter law governs.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Residence in the LS country does not displace the stricter applicable MS law.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Exhibit 1: 'Member resides in LS country, does business in MS country; MS law applies. Duties: Member must adhere to the law of MS country. Explanation: Because applicable law is stricter than the Code and Standards, member must adhere to the stricter applicable law.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 Allen*: senior manager tells him financial statements filed with regulator overstate earnings; brokerage's general counsel says difficult for regulator to prove involvement. *Most likely*:

options.A: although recommended that members and candidates seek advice of legal counsel, reliance on such advice does NOT absolve a member or candidate from requirement to comply with law or regulation.
options.B: Allen may rely conclusively on the general counsel's advice and take no further action.
options.C: Allen has no obligation because he did not prepare the misstated financial statements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: although recommended that members and candidates seek advice of legal counsel, reliance on such advice does NOT absolve a member or candidate from requirement to comply with law or regulation; Allen should report situation to his supervisor, seek an independent legal opinion, and determine whether regulator should be notified of error.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Although it is recommended that members and candidates seek the advice of legal counsel, the reliance on such advice does not absolve a member or candidate from the requirement to comply with the law or regulation. Allen should report this situation to his supervisor, seek an independent legal opinion, and determine whether the regulator should be notified of the error.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Seeking counsel is recommended, but reliance on advice does not absolve compliance with I(A); he must address the violation and, if needed, dissociate.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Knowledge of the overstatement obligates him to act regardless of authorship.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Brown*: employer is principal underwriter for Courtney convertible debentures; Brown discovers Courtney concealed severe Q3 losses in foreign operations; preliminary prospectus already distributed. *Most likely* Brown must:

options.A: wait for regulator.
options.B: report findings to appropriate supervisory persons in his firm.
options.C: let it slide.

rationales.A: Report → dissociate if unresolved.
rationales.B:
Full statement: report findings to appropriate supervisory persons in his firm; if matter is not remedied and Brown's employer does not dissociate from the underwriting, Brown must sever all his connections with the underwriting; should also seek legal advice to determine whether additional reporting or other action should be taken.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Knowing that the preliminary prospectus is misleading, Brown should report his findings to the appropriate supervisory persons in his firm. If the matter is not remedied and Brown's employer does not dissociate from the underwriting, Brown must sever all his connections with the underwriting. Brown should also seek legal advice to determine whether additional reporting or other action should be taken.'
rationales.C: Dissociate.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Washington*: composite omits accounts that left firm during 10-year period, inflating performance; asked to use erroneous number in solicitation. *Most likely* Washington:

options.A: Washington may use the number in solicitations because she did not calculate the composite herself.
options.B: Washington faces no exposure so long as the survivorship omission is disclosed in a footnote.
options.C: will assist in violating Standard I(A) if she uses inflated performance number when soliciting clients even though she did not calculate performance herself.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Knowingly using an inflated figure ASSISTS the violation — prohibited participation under I(A).
rationales.B: Incorrect. Survivorship-biased performance is misleading; fine print does not cure knowing use. She must dissociate, escalating and refusing to use the number.
rationales.C:
Full statement: will assist in violating Standard I(A) if she uses inflated performance number when soliciting clients even though she did not calculate performance herself; must dissociate herself from the activity; if discussing misleading number with responsible person is not option, must bring to attention of supervisor or compliance department; if firm unwilling to recalculate, must refrain from using misleading promotional material; if firm insists, should consider whether obligation to dissociate requires her to seek other employment.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Misrepresenting performance is a violation of the Code and Standards. Although she did not calculate the performance herself, Washington will assist in violating Standard I(A) if she were to use the inflated performance number when soliciting clients. She must dissociate herself from the activity. If discussing the misleading number with the person responsible is not an option for correcting the problem, she must bring the situation to the attention of her supervisor or the compliance department at her firm. If her firm is unwilling to recalculate performance, she must refrain from using the misleading promotional material and should notify the firm of her reasons. If the firm insists that she use the material, she should consider whether her obligation to dissociate from the activity requires her to seek other employment.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Collins*: works in developing country with minimal securities laws (no punitive prohibitions against insider trading). *Most likely* Collins:

options.A: must abide by requirements of Code and Standards, which are stricter than rules of developing country.
options.B: may use MNPI locally.
options.C: law of nation is only requirement.

rationales.A:
Full statement: must abide by requirements of Code and Standards, which are stricter than rules of developing country; in handling material nonpublic information that comes into his possession, he must follow Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Collins must abide by the requirements of the Code and Standards, which are stricter than the rules of the developing country. In handling material nonpublic information that comes into his possession, he must follow Standard II(A) Material Nonpublic Information.'
rationales.B: Stricter C&S wins.
rationales.C: C&S applies.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Jameson*: Karramba law prohibits registered investment advisers from participating in IPOs; Jameson (US company, US citizen) ignores Karrambian law and participates in IPOs. *Most likely*:

options.A: US law prevails.
options.B: Jameson is in violation of Standard I(A).
options.C: no violation.

rationales.A: Stricter local law wins.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Jameson is in violation of Standard I(A); as registered investment adviser in Karramba, Jameson is prevented by Karrambian securities law from participating in IPOs regardless of law of her home country; because law of country where she works is STRICTER than Code and Standards, she must follow the stricter requirements of the local law rather than the requirements of the Code and Standards.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Jameson is in violation of Standard I(A). As a registered investment adviser in Karramba, Jameson is prevented by Karrambian securities law from participating in IPOs regardless of the law of her home country. In addition, because the law of the country where she works is stricter than the Code and Standards, she must follow the stricter requirements of the local law rather than the requirements of the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: Local stricter law.

questions.stem: *Example 6 White*: uses social media without keeping up with new local regulator requirements on online electronic communication. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because social media is personal communication outside regulatory scope.
options.B: White violated Standard I(C) by misrepresenting her qualifications online.
options.C: White is in violation of Standard I(A) because her communications do not comply with existing applicable regulation governing use of social media.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members must keep current with and comply with regulations governing professional communications, including online channels.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The failure is noncompliance with communication regulation under I(A), not a qualifications misrepresentation.
rationales.C:
Full statement: White is in violation of Standard I(A) because her communications do not comply with existing applicable regulation governing use of social media; must be aware of evolving legal requirements pertaining to areas of financial services industry that apply to her; should seek guidance from appropriate, knowledgeable, and reliable sources such as her firm's compliance department, external service providers, or outside counsel, unless she diligently follows legal and regulatory trends affecting her professional responsibilities; having appropriate knowledge of laws directly applicable to her professional activities is also required by Standard I(E) Competence.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'White is in violation of Standard I(A) because her communications do not comply with the existing applicable regulation governing use of social media. White must be aware of the evolving legal requirements pertaining to areas of the financial services industry that apply to her. She should seek guidance from appropriate, knowledgeable, and reliable sources... Having appropriate knowledge of the laws directly applicable to her professional activities is also required by Standard I(E) Competence.'

questions.stem: *Example 7 Scherzer*: portfolio manager assisting in firm's annual financial audit; primary responsibility is portfolio management. *Most likely*:

options.A: although Scherzer assists with the audit, his primary professional responsibilities relate to portfolio management of client accounts.
options.B: Scherzer violated Standard I(A) because assisting with an audit requires mastery of all applicable audit regulations.
options.C: Scherzer must resign from the audit assignment to avoid violating the competence standard.

rationales.A:
Full statement: although Scherzer assists with the audit, his primary professional responsibilities relate to portfolio management of client accounts; as such, Standard I(A) does not require Scherzer to have detailed knowledge of the financial audit regulations applicable to his firm; Strasberg, as CFO and person responsible for internal audit team, would be required by Standard I(A) to understand and comply with audit regulations; Scherzer can rely on Strasberg to understand what information is required by regulations and give him advice on how to properly gather and disclose that information.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: '...his primary professional responsibilities relate to portfolio management of client accounts. As such, Standard I(A) does not require Scherzer to have detailed knowledge of the financial audit regulations applicable to his firm. Strasberg, as the CFO and the person responsible for the internal audit team, would be required by Standard I(A) to understand and comply with the audit regulations. Scherzer can rely on Strasberg to understand what information is required by the regulations and give him advice on how to properly gather and disclose that information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. His primary responsibilities are portfolio management; detailed audit-law expertise is not required for an assisting role.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Assisting within one's role is permissible; no resignation is called for.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Taylor*: Precision Metals mining tour, chartered group flights (analysts not expected to pay), modest Spartan Motels for 3 nights; Adams pays own hotel. *Most likely*:

options.A: Taylor and the other analysts clearly violated Standard I(B) by accepting the chartered flight and lodging.
options.B: Adams's policy complies closely with Standard I(B) by avoiding even the appearance of conflict, but Taylor and other analysts did NOT necessarily violate Standard I(B).
options.C: Adams violated Standard I(B) by paying his own expenses, since doing so implies the others acted improperly.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Modest, business-purpose arrangements for a remote site (group charter, modest motel) are a judgment call, not a per se violation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Adams's policy complies closely with Standard I(B) by avoiding even the appearance of conflict, but Taylor and other analysts did NOT necessarily violate Standard I(B); when allowing companies to pay for travel/accommodations in these circumstances, members and candidates must use judgment; here the trip was strictly for business, Taylor was not accepting irrelevant or lavish hospitality, itinerary required chartered flights for which analysts were not expected to pay, accommodations were modest; members and candidates must consider whether they can remain objective and whether integrity might be perceived by clients to have been compromised.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'The policy of the company where Adams works complies closely with Standard I(B) by avoiding even the appearance of a conflict of interest, but Taylor and the other analysts did not necessarily violate Standard I(B). In general, when allowing companies to pay for travel and/or accommodations in these circumstances, members and candidates must use their judgment... In this example, the trip was strictly for business and Taylor was not accepting irrelevant or lavish hospitality. The itinerary required chartered flights, for which analysts were not expected to pay. The accommodations were modest. In the final analysis, members and candidates must consider both whether they can remain objective and whether their integrity might be perceived by their clients to have been compromised.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Paying one's own way is best-practice compliance, not a violation.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Dillon*: corporate finance analyst promises potential new business client full research coverage. *Most likely*:

options.A: Dillon may promise favorable research coverage since corporate finance and research are separate departments.
options.B: Dillon must refuse to discuss the possibility of research coverage in any form with a prospective client.
options.C: Dillon MAY agree to provide research coverage but must NOT commit her firm's research department to providing a FAVORABLE recommendation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Promising a FAVORABLE recommendation compromises research independence regardless of departmental separation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Agreeing to provide coverage — without committing to its conclusion — is permissible.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Dillon MAY agree to provide research coverage but must NOT commit her firm's research department to providing a FAVORABLE recommendation; firm's recommendation (favorable, neutral, or unfavorable) must be based on independent and objective investigation and analysis of company and its securities.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Dillon may agree to provide research coverage, but she must not commit her firm's research department to providing a favorable recommendation. The firm's recommendation (favorable, neutral, or unfavorable) must be based on an independent and objective investigation and analysis of the company and its securities.'

questions.stem: *Example 3 Fritz (intrafirm pressure)*: senior manager sends Fritz proposal for Metals & Mining debt underwriting when he's concluded stock overpriced. *Most likely*:

options.A: Fritz's analysis of Metals & Mining must be objective and based solely on consideration of company fundamentals.
options.B: should soften rating.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Fritz's analysis of Metals & Mining must be objective and based solely on consideration of company fundamentals; any pressure from other divisions of his firm is inappropriate; this conflict could have been eliminated if, in anticipation of the offering, Hilton Brokerage had placed Metals & Mining on a RESTRICTED LIST.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Fritz's analysis of Metals & Mining must be objective and based solely on consideration of company fundamentals. Any pressure from other divisions of his firm is inappropriate. This conflict could have been eliminated if, in anticipation of the offering, Hilton Brokerage had placed Metals & Mining on a restricted list.'
rationales.B: Objective + restricted list.
rationales.C: Objective analysis.

questions.stem: *Example 5 Warner*: bond department support role, comp linked to dept performance; asked to push Milton bonds firm can't sell after operating-problem announcement. *Most likely* Warner must:

options.A: push bonds.
options.B: unethical sales practices create significant potential violations of Code and Standards.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Refuse to push.
rationales.B:
Full statement: unethical sales practices create significant potential violations of Code and Standards; Warner's opinion of Milton bonds must NOT be affected by internal pressure or compensation; in this case, Warner must REFUSE to push Milton bonds unless she is able to justify that market price has already adjusted for the operating problem.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'Unethical sales practices create significant potential violations of the Code and Standards. Warner's opinion of the Milton bonds must not be affected by internal pressure or compensation. In this case, Warner must refuse to push the Milton bonds unless she is able to justify that the market price has already adjusted for the operating problem.'
rationales.C: Refuse.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Jorund*: boss instructs her that under no circumstances should prevailing buy recommendation on International Airlines be changed. *Most likely* Jorund:

options.A: must obey.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: must be independent and objective.

rationales.A: Two options.
rationales.B: Independent opinion.
rationales.C:
Full statement: must be independent and objective; if she believes her boss's instructions have compromised her, has two options: tell boss she cannot cover company under these constraints, OR take over coverage, reach her own independent conclusions, and if they conflict with boss's opinion share the conclusions with boss or other supervisors so they can make appropriate recommendations; must issue only recommendations that reflect her independent and objective opinion.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Jorund must be independent and objective in her analysis of International Airlines. If she believes that her boss's instructions have compromised her, she has two options: She can tell her boss that she cannot cover the company under these constraints, or she can take over coverage of the company, reach her own independent conclusions, and if they conflict with her boss's opinion, share the conclusions with her boss or other supervisors in the firm so that they can make appropriate recommendations. Jorund must issue only recommendations that reflect her independent and objective opinion.'

questions.stem: *Example 7 Grant*: directs commission business to NY brokerage; brokerage gives Grant two tickets to World Cup in South Africa, two nights resort, meals, limousine. *Most likely*:

options.A: Grant violated Standard I(B) because accepting these substantial gifts may impede his independence and objectivity.
options.B: no violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Grant violated Standard I(B) because accepting these substantial gifts may impede his independence and objectivity; members and candidates must not solicit or accept gifts, contributions, or other compensation that affects their independence and objectivity or that reasonably could be expected to influence their decision-making process; by accepting the trip Grant created conflict of interest and opened himself up to accusation that he may give the broker favored treatment in return; at minimum, Grant must disclose this conflict to his employer and clients.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Grant violated Standard I(B) because accepting these substantial gifts may impede his independence and objectivity. Members and candidates must not solicit or accept gifts, contributions, or other compensation that affects their independence and objectivity or that reasonably could be expected to influence their decision-making process... By accepting the trip, Grant created a conflict of interest and opened himself up to the accusation that he may give the broker favored treatment in return. At a minimum, Grant must disclose this conflict to his employer and clients.'
rationales.B: Violation — substantial gifts.
rationales.C: Substantial gifts violate.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Green*: consistently beats benchmark for Knowlden; Knowlden offers Wimbledon tickets + London flat use; Green discloses to Tisbury supervisor. *Most likely*:

options.A: gifts prohibited from clients.
options.B: Green is in compliance with Standard I(B) because she disclosed the gift from one of her clients.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Client gift OK if disclosed.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Green is in compliance with Standard I(B) because she disclosed the gift from one of her clients; members and candidates MAY accept bonuses or gifts from clients as long as they disclose them to their employer because gifts in a client relationship are deemed less likely to affect independence than gifts in other situations; disclosure required so supervisors can monitor to guard against employees favoring gift-giving client to detriment of other fee-paying clients (e.g., by allocating greater proportion of IPO stock to gift-giving client's portfolio).

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'Green is in compliance with Standard I(B) because she disclosed the gift from one of her clients. Members and candidates may accept bonuses or gifts from clients as long as they disclose them to their employer because gifts in a client relationship are deemed less likely to affect a member's or candidate's objectivity and independence than gifts in other situations. Disclosure is required, however, so that supervisors can monitor such situations to guard against employees favoring a gift-giving client to the detriment of other fee-paying clients (such as by allocating a greater proportion of IPO stock to the gift-giving client's portfolio).'
rationales.C: Client gift + disclose.

questions.stem: *Example 9 Wayne*: pension plan manager recommends Penguin Advisers; Penguin was sponsor of 'investment fact-finding trip to Asia' Wayne made where Academy paid travel/meals/accommodations, in daily company of Penguin president. *Most likely*:

options.A: Wayne's recommendation is untainted because the trip was educational and improved his manager-selection knowledge.
options.B: Wayne violated Standard III(A) Loyalty, Prudence, and Care merely by attending the sponsored trip.
options.C: although Wayne can probably use knowledge gained when selecting external managers, his recommendation of Penguin Advisers may be TAINTED by possible conflict incurred when he participated in international trip partly paid for by Penguin Advisers and was in daily company of Penguin's president.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Lavish sponsor-paid travel, meals, and accommodations create a conflict that may taint the subsequent recommendation despite any educational value.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The concern analyzed is independence and objectivity under I(B); attendance itself is not the violation — the compromised recommendation is.
rationales.C:
Full statement: although Wayne can probably use knowledge gained when selecting external managers, his recommendation of Penguin Advisers may be TAINTED by possible conflict incurred when he participated in international trip partly paid for by Penguin Advisers and was in daily company of Penguin's president; to avoid violating Standard I(B), Wayne's basic expenses for travel and accommodations should have been paid by his employer or the pension plan; contact with Penguin president should have been limited to informational or educational events only; and the trip, organizer, and sponsor should have been made a matter of public record.

Correct. Per Example 9 Comment: 'Although Wayne can probably put to good use the knowledge he gained from the trip when selecting external portfolio managers and in other areas of managing the pension plan, his recommendation of Penguin Advisers may be tainted by the possible conflict incurred when he participated in the international trip partly paid for by Penguin Advisers and when he was in the daily company of the president of Penguin Advisers. To avoid violating Standard I(B), Wayne's basic expenses for travel and accommodations should have been paid by his employer or the pension plan; contact with the president of Penguin Advisers should have been limited to informational or educational events only; and the trip, the organizer, and the sponsor should have been made a matter of public record.'

questions.stem: *Example 10 Herrero*: hired by investor relations firm to write research report; flat fee + BONUS if new investors buy stock from his report. *Most likely*:

options.A: if Herrero accepts payment arrangement, he will be in violation of Standard I(B) because compensation arrangement can reasonably be expected to compromise his independence and objectivity.
options.B: The arrangement is acceptable because the bonus depends on investors' buying decisions, not on Herrero's stated conclusions.
options.C: Herrero may accept the arrangement provided the bonus is capped at a small percentage of the flat fee.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if Herrero accepts payment arrangement, he will be in violation of Standard I(B) because compensation arrangement can reasonably be expected to compromise his independence and objectivity; Herrero will receive bonus for attracting investors, which provides incentive to draft positive report regardless of facts and to ignore/downplay any negative information; should accept only flat fee that is NOT tied to conclusions or recommendations of the report; issuer-paid research that is objective and unbiased is acceptable under certain circumstances as long as analyst takes steps to maintain objectivity and includes in report proper disclosures regarding potential conflicts of interest.

Correct. Per Example 10 Comment: 'If Herrero accepts this payment arrangement, he will be in violation of Standard I(B) because the compensation arrangement can reasonably be expected to compromise his independence and objectivity. Herrero will receive a bonus for attracting investors, which provides an incentive to draft a positive report regardless of the facts and to ignore or play down any negative information about the company. Herrero should accept only a flat fee that is not tied to the conclusions or recommendations of the report. Issuer-paid research that is objective and unbiased is acceptable under certain circumstances as long as the analyst takes steps to maintain his or her objectivity and includes in the report proper disclosures regarding potential conflicts of interest.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Compensation contingent on report-driven buying can reasonably be expected to compromise independence and objectivity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The size of the contingent payment does not cure the conflict; the structure itself is the problem.

questions.stem: *Example 11 Mandel*: makes significant contributions to Gomez's UDPBU union reelection campaign fund; lavishly entertains Gomez and family; outlays handled as ZZYY marketing expenses and disclosed to senior management. *Most likely*:

options.A: No violation occurred because the outlays were recorded as marketing expenses and disclosed to ZZYY's management.
options.B: Mandel not only offered but actually GAVE monetary gifts, benefits, and other considerations that reasonably could be expected to compromise Gomez's objectivity.
options.C: Mandel's conduct is governed solely by union election law and falls outside the Code's scope.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Internal accounting treatment and employer disclosure do not cure gifts intended to compromise another party's objectivity.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Mandel not only offered but actually GAVE monetary gifts, benefits, and other considerations that reasonably could be expected to compromise Gomez's objectivity; therefore, Mandel was in violation of Standard I(B); Standard applies to giving as well as receiving gifts and benefits — attempting to compromise ANOTHER'S independence and objectivity is prohibited.

Correct. Per Example 11 Comment: 'Mandel not only offered but actually gave monetary gifts, benefits, and other considerations that reasonably could be expected to compromise Gomez's objectivity. Therefore, Mandel was in violation of Standard I(B).' Standard I(B) prohibits offering, soliciting, or accepting gifts that could compromise 'their own or another's independence and objectivity.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Giving benefits that compromise someone's independence and objectivity is squarely within Standard I(B).

questions.stem: *Example 12 Scott*: notices external manager changed composite construction to conceal bad performance of large account; manager is close personal friend of firm's CEO. *Most likely*:

options.A: may omit.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Scott would be in violation of Standard I(B) if she did not disclose the change in her final report.

rationales.A: Must disclose the change.
rationales.B: Disclose.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Scott would be in violation of Standard I(B) if she did not disclose the change in her final report; analysis of managers' performance must NOT be influenced by personal relationships or size of allocation to outside managers; by not including the change, Scott would not be providing independent analysis of performance metrics for her firm.

Correct. Per Example 12 Comment: 'Scott would be in violation of Standard I(B) if she did not disclose the change in her final report. The analysis of managers' performance must not be influenced by personal relationships or the size of the allocation to the outside managers. By not including the change, Scott would not be providing an independent analysis of the performance metrics for her firm.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 2 Ward*: expenses (meals, hotel, air) offered by Evans Industries. Best course under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: pay for all travel expenses, including costs of meals and incidental items.
options.B: Accept + write objective report.
options.C: Accept + disclose value in report.

rationales.A:
Full statement: pay for all travel expenses, including costs of meals and incidental items; best course of action under Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity is to AVOID a conflict of interest whenever possible; option C (accept + disclose) is not as appropriate as avoiding the conflict; option A does not remove the appearance of a conflict — even though report would not be affected, could appear to be.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 2 Solution: 'The best course of action under Standard I(B) Independence and Objectivity is to avoid a conflict of interest whenever possible. Therefore, for Ward to pay for all his expenses is the correct answer. Option C details a course of action in which the conflict would be disclosed, but this solution is not as appropriate as avoiding the conflict of interest. Option A would not be the best course because it would not remove the appearance of a conflict of interest; even though the report would not be affected by the reimbursement of expenses, it could appear to be.'
rationales.B: Pay own expenses.
rationales.C: Pay own — avoid better than disclose.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 3 ETV*: head of investment banking asks head of brokerage unit to change 'sell' to 'buy'; ETV is being taken through additional public offering. Permitted action *most likely*:

options.A: Increase by one increment (to hold).
options.B: place the company on a restricted list and give only factual information about the company.
options.C: Assign new analyst.

rationales.A: Restricted list + factual only.
rationales.B:
Full statement: place the company on a restricted list and give only factual information about the company; head of brokerage unit must REFUSE to yield to pressure from investment banking department to maintain independence and objectivity in making recommendations; to avoid appearance of conflict of interest, firm should discontinue issuing recommendations about the company; changing recommendation in ANY manner contrary to analyst's opinion violates duty; assigning new analyst to decide whether stock deserves higher rating will not address conflict of interest.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 3 Solution: 'When asked to change a recommendation on a company stock to gain business for the firm, the head of the brokerage unit must refuse in order to maintain his independence and objectivity in making recommendations. He must not yield to pressure by the firm's investment banking department. To avoid the appearance of a conflict of interest, the firm should discontinue issuing recommendations about the company. A is incorrect; changing the recommendation in any manner that is contrary to the analyst's opinion violates the duty to maintain independence and objectivity. C is incorrect because merely assigning a new analyst to decide whether the stock deserves a higher rating will not address the conflict of interest.'
rationales.C: Restricted list.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 4 Long*: conference host offers (1) all conference/travel costs, (2) free registration for 3 additional team members, (3) exclusive golf outing at championship course next day. Least likely to violate I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: may accept only offer to have his conference-related expenses paid by host firm; accepting exclusive golf outing (which is not conference-related and is lavish) or full package would violate I(B); modest business-related travel/accommodations for the conference itself may be acceptable but lavish extras such as exclusive golf outings are not.
options.B: Accept full package.
options.C: Accept only conference-related expenses paid by host firm.

rationales.A: Conference expenses only.
rationales.B: Full package violates.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Practice Problem 4: option A (conference-related expenses only) is least likely to violate I(B). The exclusive golf outing at a championship course is a lavish extra unrelated to the professional purpose and would create a conflict of interest that could compromise Long's independence and objectivity in evaluating the manager.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Rogers*: partner in small advisory firm assures prospect 'we can perform all the financial and investment services you need' when firm cannot provide tax planning. *Most likely*:

options.A: Rogers violated Standard I(C) by orally misrepresenting the services her firm can perform for prospective client.
options.B: no violation.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Rogers violated Standard I(C) by orally misrepresenting the services her firm can perform for prospective client; using vague, imprecise, and general language such as 'all the financial and investment services you need' oversells and misrepresents services she and her firm can provide; must limit herself to describing range of investment advisory services Rogers and Black can provide and offer to help client obtain elsewhere the financial and investment services that her firm cannot provide.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Rogers violated Standard I(C) by orally misrepresenting the services her firm can perform for the prospective client. Using vague, imprecise, and general language such as "all the financial and investment services you need" oversells and misrepresents the services she and her firm can provide. She must limit herself to describing the range of investment advisory services Rogers and Black can provide and offer to help the client obtain elsewhere the financial and investment services that her firm cannot provide.'
rationales.B: Vague overselling violates.
rationales.C: Overselling violates.

questions.stem: *Example 2 McGuire*: issuer-paid analyst with website promoting research as seemingly independent; posts profiles and strong buy recommendations without disclosing contractual relationships. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation.
options.B: McGuire violated Standard I(C) because internet site is misleading to potential investors.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: I(C) + VI(A) violations.
rationales.B:
Full statement: McGuire violated Standard I(C) because internet site is misleading to potential investors; even if recommendations are valid and supported with thorough research, his omissions regarding true relationship between himself and companies he covers constitute misrepresentation; McGuire also violated Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts by not disclosing existence of arrangement with companies from which he receives compensation in exchange for his services.

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'McGuire violated Standard I(C) because the internet site is misleading to potential investors. Even if the recommendations are valid and supported with thorough research, his omissions regarding the true relationship between himself and the companies he covers constitute a misrepresentation. McGuire also violated Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts by not disclosing the existence of an arrangement with the companies from which he receives compensation in exchange for his services.'
rationales.C: Two violations.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Yao*: creates presentation with typo showing composite has ¥350 billion when actual is ¥35 billion; distributed before catches error. *Most likely*:

options.A: Yao violated Standard I(C) because the presentation misstated the composite's size by a factor of ten.
options.B: No corrective action is required because the error was typographical and unintentional.
options.C: once error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of incorrect material and correct error by informing those who received erroneous information.

rationales.A: Incorrect. I(C) prohibits KNOWING misrepresentation; an unintentional typo is not a violation at creation.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Once the error is discovered, distribution must cease and recipients must be informed and corrected.
rationales.C:
Full statement: once error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of incorrect material and correct error by informing those who received erroneous information; because Yao did NOT KNOWINGLY make the misrepresentation, however, he did NOT violate Standard I(C); since his firm claims compliance with GIPS standards, he must also comply with GIPS standards requirements addressing treatment of material errors.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Once the error is discovered, Yao must take steps to cease distribution of the incorrect material and correct the error by informing those who have received the erroneous information. Because Yao did not knowingly make the misrepresentation, however, he did not violate Standard I(C). Since his firm claims compliance with GIPS standards, he must also comply with the GIPS standards requirements addressing the treatment of material errors.'

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 5 Grey*: recommends US Treasury bond mutual fund with statements: (I) 'payment of bonds is guaranteed by the US government; therefore, the default risk of the bonds is virtually zero'; (II) 'if you invest in the mutual fund, you will earn a 10% rate of return each year for the next several years based on historical performance'. *Most likely*:

options.A: Only Statement II violates Code and Standards.
options.B: Neither violates.
options.C: Only Statement I violates.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Only Statement II violates Code and Standards; Standard I(C) prohibits members and candidates from stating or implying that clients will obtain or achieve rate of return that was generated in past — such guarantees are misleading to investors; Statement I is factually accurate — US Treasury bonds are backed by full faith and credit of US government so default risk on the bonds themselves is minimal.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 5 background and Standard I(C) guidance: 'Standard I(C) prohibits members and candidates from stating or implying that clients will obtain or achieve a rate of return that was generated in the past. Guaranteeing either a particular rate of return or preservation of investment capital... is also misleading to investors and a violation of this Standard.' Statement II projects a specific return based on past performance — a violation. Statement I is a factual statement about US Treasury credit backing.
rationales.B: II only.
rationales.C: Statement II violates.

questions.stem: *Practice Problem 6 Michelieu*: 'my equity-oriented clients have averaged a total return of more than 26% a year' — statement true, but one client's large penny-stock gain (against Michelieu's advice) drove the average; without it, average would have been 8%. *Most likely*:

options.A: No — not promising future.
options.B: Yes, because statement misrepresents Michelieu's track record.
options.C: No — statement is accurate.

rationales.A: Misrepresents track record.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Yes, because statement misrepresents Michelieu's track record; even though technically true, the statement gives misleading impression of Michelieu's performance because one client's large gain (that Michelieu advised against) drove the average far above what his recommendations actually produced; misrepresentation includes creating misleading impression through selective or context-free statistics.

Correct. Per Practice Problem 6 setup and Standard I(C) guidance: Standard I(C) prohibits members and candidates from misrepresentations even if statements are technically true. The 26% figure is driven by one client's investment that Michelieu ADVISED AGAINST — so the return does not reflect his recommendations. Presenting it as evidence of his track record misrepresents his abilities.
rationales.C: Yes — misleading.

questions.stem: *Example 20 Al-Wazir (ChatGPT for AI RFP)*: uses ChatGPT to answer RFP question about firm's AI capabilities to appear more knowledgeable than he is. *Most likely*:

options.A: Using generative AI tools to draft RFP responses is always permissible and raises no Code issues.
options.B: Al-Wazir's only failing is neglecting to cite ChatGPT as a source under the plagiarism guidance.
options.C: Al-Wazir violated Standard I(C) by misrepresenting his firm's capabilities relating to AI in its investment process and using ChatGPT to respond to RFP to hide his superficial knowledge of topic and seem more knowledgeable than he is.

rationales.A: Incorrect. The tool is not the issue; using it to overstate the firm's AI capabilities and his own knowledge is misrepresentation under I(C).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The violation is the misrepresentation of capabilities and expertise, not a missing citation.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Example 20 Comment: 'Al-Wazir violated Standard I(C) by misrepresenting his firm's capabilities relating to AI in its investment process and using ChatGPT to respond to the RFP to hide his superficial knowledge of the topic and seem more knowledgeable than he is.'

questions.stem: *Plagiarism definition and examples* *most likely*:

options.A: plagiarism is defined as COPYING or USING in substantially the same form materials prepared by others without acknowledging source of material or identifying author and publisher.
options.B: Using materially the same analysis from another author is acceptable so long as the wording is paraphrased.
options.C: Plagiarism rules apply only to published academic work, not to internal research or oral statements.

rationales.A:
Full statement: plagiarism is defined as COPYING or USING in substantially the same form materials prepared by others without acknowledging source of material or identifying author and publisher; must not copy (or represent as their own) original ideas or material without permission and must acknowledge and identify source of ideas or material that is not their own; examples include: (1) taking research report by another firm/person, changing names, releasing as one's own; (2) using excerpts from articles/reports either verbatim or with only slight wording changes without acknowledgment; (3) citing specific quotations as attributable to 'leading analysts' without naming specific references; (4) presenting statistical estimates/forecasts prepared by others identifying sources but without qualifying statements/caveats; (5) using charts and graphs without stating sources; (6) copying proprietary spreadsheets or algorithms without seeking cooperation or authorization of their creators.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Plagiarism guidance: 'Plagiarism is defined as copying or using in substantially the same form materials prepared by others without acknowledging the source of the material or identifying the author and publisher of such material. Members and candidates must not copy (or represent as their own) original ideas or material without permission and must acknowledge and identify the source of ideas or material that is not their own... Other practices include (1) using excerpts from articles or reports prepared by others either verbatim or with only slight changes in wording without acknowledgment, (2) citing specific quotations as attributable to "leading analysts" and "investment experts" without naming the specific references, (3) presenting statistical estimates of forecasts prepared by others and identifying the sources but without including the qualifying statements or caveats that may have been used, (4) using charts and graphs without stating their sources, and (5) copying proprietary spreadsheets or algorithms without seeking the cooperation or authorization of their creators.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Copying or using material in SUBSTANTIALLY THE SAME FORM without attribution is plagiarism, paraphrased or not.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The prohibition covers professional materials broadly, including reports, models, and oral or electronic communications.

questions.stem: *Prior analyst departure and firm's continued use of work* *most likely*:

options.A: The firm must discard all models and research created by an analyst who has departed.
options.B: in some situations, members or candidates may use research conducted or models developed by others within same firm WITHOUT committing violation.
options.C: Continued use requires the departed analyst's written consent and ongoing personal attribution.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Work product created for the employer belongs to the firm and may continue to be used.
rationales.B:
Full statement: in some situations, members or candidates may use research conducted or models developed by others within same firm WITHOUT committing violation; most common example: one (or more) of original analysts is no longer with firm; research and models developed while employed by firm are property of the firm; firm retains right to continue using work completed after member or candidate has left organization; firm may issue future reports without providing attribution to prior analysts; member or candidate must NOT, however, reissue a previously released report solely under his or her name.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Plagiarism guidance: 'In some situations, however, members or candidates may use research conducted or models developed by others within the same firm without committing a violation. The most common example relates to the situation in which one (or more) of the original analysts is no longer with the firm. Research and models developed while employed by a firm are the property of the firm. The firm retains the right to continue using the work completed after a member or candidate has left the organization. The firm may issue future reports without providing attribution to the prior analysts. A member or candidate must not, however, reissue a previously released report solely under his or her name.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Within the firm, research and models developed by others may be used without the departed author's consent.

questions.stem: *Example 1 Sasserman*: bank trust investment officer drinks at country club lunch and returns intoxicated to make investment decisions. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because off-premises lunchtime drinking is personal conduct outside the Code's reach.
options.B: Sasserman violated Standard V(A) because intoxication impaired the reasonable basis for his decisions.
options.C: Sasserman's excessive drinking at lunch and subsequent intoxication at work constitute violation of Standard I(D) because this conduct has raised questions about his professionalism and competence.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Returning intoxicated to make investment decisions raises questions about professionalism and competence — misconduct under I(D).
rationales.B: Incorrect. The example's issue is Misconduct I(D), not the diligence and reasonable basis standard.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Sasserman's excessive drinking at lunch and subsequent intoxication at work constitute violation of Standard I(D) because this conduct has raised questions about his professionalism and competence; behavior reflects poorly on him, his employer, and the investment industry.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Sasserman's excessive drinking at lunch and subsequent intoxication at work constitute a violation of Standard I(D) because this conduct has raised questions about his professionalism and competence. His behavior reflects poorly on him, his employer, and the investment industry.'

questions.stem: *Example 2 Hoffman*: security analyst submits altered health insurance reimbursement forms to firm's self-funded program over two years; firm terminates and notifies CFA Institute. *Most likely*:

options.A: Hoffman violated Standard I(D) because he engaged in intentional conduct involving fraud and deceit in the workplace that adversely reflected on his integrity.
options.B: There is no professional violation because insurance reimbursement fraud is a personal financial matter.
options.C: Hoffman violated Standard III(A) by breaching duties owed to his clients.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Hoffman violated Standard I(D) because he engaged in intentional conduct involving fraud and deceit in the workplace that adversely reflected on his integrity.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Intentional fraud and deceit in the workplace reflects adversely on professional integrity — I(D) applies.
rationales.C: Incorrect. No client duty is implicated; the violation is Misconduct under I(D).

questions.stem: *Example 4 Garcia*: mutual fund manager arrested for trespassing during nonviolent environmental protests. *Most likely*:

options.A: Garcia violated Standard I(D) because any arrest reflects adversely on professional reputation.
options.B: generally, Standard I(D) is not meant to cover legal transgressions resulting from acts of civil disobedience in support of personal beliefs, because such conduct does not reflect poorly on the member's or candidate's professional reputation, integrity, or competence.
options.C: Garcia violated Standard I(A) by failing to comply with applicable trespassing laws.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Legal transgressions from civil disobedience generally do not involve the dishonesty, fraud, or deceit that I(D) targets.
rationales.B: Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Generally, Standard I(D) is not meant to cover legal transgressions resulting from acts of civil disobedience in support of personal beliefs, because such conduct does not reflect poorly on the member's or candidate's professional reputation, integrity, or competence.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. I(A) concerns compliance with laws governing PROFESSIONAL activities, not personal protest conduct.

questions.stem: *Example 5 O'Hara*: society volunteer instructor engages in sexual harassment of female candidates. *Most likely*:

options.A: There is no violation because volunteer teaching for a society falls outside professional activities.
options.B: The conduct violates only the society's internal rules, not the Code and Standards.
options.C: O'Hara is in violation of Standard I(D) by engaging in sexual harassment that reflects poorly on his professional reputation and integrity.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Participation in the investment profession — including society instruction — is professional activity; harassment reflects adversely on integrity.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Standard I(D) reaches the conduct directly; it is not merely a local-rules matter.
rationales.C:
Full statement: O'Hara is in violation of Standard I(D) by engaging in sexual harassment that reflects poorly on his professional reputation and integrity; although actions do not take place in the workplace, O'Hara's actions are professional conduct within the meaning of Code and Standards because conduct takes place as part of his work with the local society and encompasses the use of his charter and membership in the organization.

Correct. Per Example 5 Comment: 'O'Hara is in violation of Standard I(D) by engaging in sexual harassment that reflects poorly on his professional reputation and integrity. Although the actions do not take place in the workplace, O'Hara's actions are professional conduct within the meaning of the Code and Standards because the conduct takes place as part of his work with the local society and encompasses the use of his charter and membership in the organization.'

questions.stem: *Example 6 Nehjer*: retired but active CFA member; publishes open letter opposing casino using fabricated/altered statistics, signs as PhD and CFA charterholder. *Most likely*:

options.A: by engaging in fraud, dishonesty, and deceit in using fabricated and altered statistics to support her opinion on casino issue, Nehjer engaged in misconduct and violated Standard I(D).
options.B: As a retired member, Nehjer's public advocacy falls outside the coverage of the Code.
options.C: Nehjer violated Standard VII(B) merely by signing the letter with her CFA designation.

rationales.A:
Full statement: by engaging in fraud, dishonesty, and deceit in using fabricated and altered statistics to support her opinion on casino issue, Nehjer engaged in misconduct and violated Standard I(D); although she is retired, her actions in this case are professional conduct within meaning of standard because she identified herself as CFA charterholder and explicitly used her charter in attempt to give her analysis and opinions more credibility.

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'By engaging in fraud, dishonesty, and deceit in using fabricated and altered statistics in an effort to support her opinion on the casino issue, Nehjer engaged in misconduct and violated Standard I(D). Although she is retired, her actions in this case are professional conduct within the meaning of the standard because she identified herself as a CFA charterholder and explicitly used her charter in an attempt to give her analysis and opinions more credibility.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Retirement does not suspend the Code for a member; fabricating and altering statistics is fraud and deceit under I(D).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Truthfully referencing the designation is permitted; the violation lies in the fabricated data, not the signature.

questions.stem: *Example 7 Mewis*: writes ESG blog with employer permission; posts false claims about JKL (personal dispute with ex-husband CEO) claiming imminent regulatory action and litigation without knowledge of any truth. *Most likely*:

options.A: Because the blog was written with employer permission, no professional-conduct issue arises.
options.B: Mewis violated Standard I(D) by engaging in dishonesty, fraud, and deceit and in conduct that reflects adversely on her professional reputation and integrity.
options.C: Mewis's statements are protected opinion because they appeared on a personal ESG blog.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Permission to blog does not authorize knowingly false claims; dishonesty and deceit violate I(D).
rationales.B:
Full statement: Mewis violated Standard I(D) by engaging in dishonesty, fraud, and deceit and in conduct that reflects adversely on her professional reputation and integrity; although her actions are not related to her employment with investment adviser, her conduct relates to candidate's involvement in investment profession and security markets.

Correct. Per Example 7 Comment: 'Mewis violated Standard I(D) by engaging in dishonesty, fraud, and deceit and in conduct that reflects adversely on her professional reputation and integrity. Although her actions are not related to her employment with the investment adviser, her conduct relates to the candidate's involvement in the investment profession and security markets.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Fabricated factual assertions — imminent regulatory action and litigation — are not opinion.

questions.stem: *Example 8 Tan*: junior analyst submits false documentation to government to obtain disabled parking permit. *Most likely*:

options.A: Tan violated Standard I(D) because submitting false documents involves fraud and deceit.
options.B: Tan violated Standard I(A) by breaking government documentation laws.
options.C: while Tan's actions involve dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and lying to government officials, his actions do NOT fall within definition of professional conduct for purposes of application of Code and Standards.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Though dishonest, obtaining a parking permit is not PROFESSIONAL conduct as I(D) defines it.
rationales.B: Incorrect. I(A) addresses laws governing professional activities; this personal misconduct falls outside it.
rationales.C:
Full statement: while Tan's actions involve dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and lying to government officials, his actions do NOT fall within definition of professional conduct for purposes of application of Code and Standards; his conduct is at most tangential to his employment and does not implicate his participation in CFA Program or any other affiliation with CFA Institute; as a result, while his conduct is deplorable, it is not 'professional' conduct to which Code and Standards apply.

Correct. Per Example 8 Comment: 'While Tan's actions involve dishonesty, fraud, deceit, and lying to government officials, his actions do not fall within the definition of professional conduct for the purposes of application of the Code and Standards. His conduct is, at most, tangential to his employment and does not implicate his participation in the CFA Program or any other affiliation with CFA Institute. As a result, while his conduct is deplorable, it is not "professional" conduct to which the Code and Standards apply.'

questions.stem: *Example 1 Lee*: geopolitical consultant closely monitors news about new multinational trade deal; upon ratification downloads copy, reads it, consults experts. *Most likely*:

options.A: Lee satisfied requirement of Standard I(E).
options.B: insufficient.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Lee satisfied requirement of Standard I(E); Lee was able to write knowledgeable and informative research report for her clients by keeping abreast of developments in her field through news reports, consulting with experts, and studying trade deal itself to ensure that her knowledge was up to date.

Correct. Per Example 1 Comment: 'Lee satisfied the requirement of Standard I(E). Lee was able to write a knowledgeable and informative research report for her clients by keeping abreast of developments in her field through news reports, consulting with experts, and studying the trade deal itself to ensure that her knowledge was up to date.'
rationales.B: Competence satisfied.
rationales.C: Competence maintained.

questions.stem: *Example 2 Choe*: clients ask for ESG ratings in research reports (traditionally only quant metrics); hires experienced ESG analyst and requires all research analysts to attend ESG seminars and obtain ESG investment specialist certificate. *Most likely*:

options.A: Choe violated Standard I(E) by initially lacking ESG expertise when clients first requested it.
options.B: Choe extended her competence in her field by educating herself on new considerations relevant to her industry and by hiring someone with ESG expertise to fill a knowledge and skills gap before she and her team incorporated ESG considerations into firm's research reports.
options.C: Choe should have declined the ESG requests, since extending beyond quantitative metrics exceeded her established mandate.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Recognizing a knowledge gap and closing it through hiring, training, and certification is precisely how competence is extended.
rationales.B:
Full statement: Choe extended her competence in her field by educating herself on new considerations relevant to her industry and by hiring someone with ESG expertise to fill a knowledge and skills gap before she and her team incorporated ESG considerations into firm's research reports; Choe satisfied requirement of Standard I(E).

Correct. Per Example 2 Comment: 'Choe extended her competence in her field by educating herself on new considerations relevant to her industry and by hiring someone with ESG expertise to fill a knowledge and skills gap before she and her team incorporated ESG considerations into the firm's research reports. As such, Choe satisfied the requirement of Standard I(E).'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Extending services while building the necessary competence is compliant conduct, not a mandate breach.

questions.stem: *Example 3 Glusker*: regulatory attorney transitions to sales role; passes licensing exam but did not learn material — relied on industry knowledge. *Most likely*:

options.A: passed — competent.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: Glusker failed to satisfy requirement of Standard I(E).

rationales.A: Passing test not enough.
rationales.B: Not competent.
rationales.C:
Full statement: Glusker failed to satisfy requirement of Standard I(E); he did receive passing score on licensing examination, but passing the examination by itself is NOT adequate to demonstrate competency; Glusker's failure to learn applicable rules and regulations relating to fairness when dealing with clients and prospects means that he does not possess required knowledge to serve in his new role, despite passing licensing examination.

Correct. Per Example 3 Comment: 'Glusker failed to satisfy the requirement of Standard I(E). He did receive a passing score on a licensing examination, but passing the examination, by itself, is not adequate to demonstrate competency. Glusker's failure to learn the applicable rules and regulations relating to fairness when dealing with clients and prospects means that he does not possess the required knowledge to serve in his new role, despite passing the licensing examination.'

questions.stem: *Example 4 Evans*: analyst promoted to director of research with supervisory responsibility for 4 analysts; spends time on research projects but no time on managing employees, leading teams, or compliance requirements relating to subordinates. *Most likely*:

options.A: Evans violated Standard I(E).
options.B: no requirement to extend.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: Evans violated Standard I(E); her new position demands competence in other skills, abilities, and knowledge beyond those needed for her previous position; Evans must ensure she extends her competence to cover all responsibilities of her new role; must obtain skills and knowledge needed for fulfilling her supervisory responsibility of other analysts — could include attending conferences or taking professional development courses on management, earning compliance credentials, and internal training on supervisory responsibility.

Correct. Per Example 4 Comment: 'Evans violated Standard I(E). Her new position demands competence in other skills, abilities, and knowledge beyond those needed for her previous position. Evans must ensure she extends her competence to cover all responsibilities of her new role. She also must now obtain the skills and knowledge needed for fulfilling her supervisory responsibility of the other analysts. This could include attending conferences or taking professional development courses on management, earning compliance credentials, and internal training on supervisory responsibility.'
rationales.B: Must extend to new role.
rationales.C: Extend competence.

questions.stem: *Example 6 Halsey*: clients demand cryptocurrency investments; Halsey unfamiliar with asset nature or differences between products but hastily purchases heavily-promoted crypto product to avoid seeming out of touch. *Most likely*:

options.A: no violation.
options.B: given his limited knowledge, Halsey is not competent to invest in cryptocurrency for his clients.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Violation — not competent.
rationales.B:
Full statement: given his limited knowledge, Halsey is not competent to invest in cryptocurrency for his clients; by hastily investing in cryptocurrency products without understanding nature of investment or suitability of investment for his clients, Halsey violated Standard I(E).

Correct. Per Example 6 Comment: 'Given his limited knowledge, Halsey is not competent to invest in cryptocurrency for his clients. By hastily investing in cryptocurrency products without understanding the nature of the investment or the suitability of the investment for his clients, Halsey violated Standard I(E).'
rationales.C: Insufficient knowledge = I(E) violation.

questions.stem: *Failure to achieve competence — unsuccessful outcome* *most likely*:

options.A: Any unsuccessful investment outcome is presumptive evidence of a competence violation.
options.B: Competence is measured solely by portfolio performance relative to the benchmark.
options.C: while competence allows members and candidates opportunity to undertake an activity successfully, lack of competence CANNOT necessarily be determined by an unsuccessful or negative outcome.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Lack of competence cannot necessarily be inferred from an unsuccessful outcome.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Competence concerns knowledge, skills, and abilities applied to the role, not realized relative returns.
rationales.C:
Full statement: while competence allows members and candidates opportunity to undertake an activity successfully, lack of competence CANNOT necessarily be determined by an unsuccessful or negative outcome; many competent investment professionals have experienced failure or loss — competent investment manager may not always make profitable investment decisions; competent securities analyst may not accurately predict future prospects of investment despite diligent and thorough analysis.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'While competence allows members and candidates the opportunity to undertake an activity successfully, lack of competence cannot necessarily be determined by an unsuccessful or a negative outcome. Many competent investment professionals have experienced failure or loss in their professional lives. For example, a competent investment manager may not always make profitable investment decisions. A competent securities analyst may not accurately predict the future prospects of an investment, despite diligent and thorough analysis.'

drills.los_text: recommend practices and procedures designed to prevent violations of the Code and Standards

questions.stem: *'More strict' law definition* under Standard I(A) *most likely*:

options.A: 'more strict' law or regulation is the law or regulation that imposes greater restrictions on action of member or candidate OR calls for member or candidate to exert greater degree of action to protect interests of clients.
options.B: 'More strict' means whichever law carries the larger monetary penalties for violations.
options.C: 'More strict' always refers to the law of the member's home country.

rationales.A:
Full statement: 'more strict' law or regulation is the law or regulation that imposes greater restrictions on action of member or candidate OR calls for member or candidate to exert greater degree of action to protect interests of clients; applicable law is law that governs member's or candidate's conduct; which law applies depends on particular facts and circumstances of each case.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Applicable law is the law that governs the member's or candidate's conduct. Which law applies will depend on the particular facts and circumstances of each case. The "more strict" law or regulation is the law or regulation that imposes greater restrictions on the action of the member or candidate or calls for the member or candidate to exert a greater degree of action to protect the interests of clients.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Strictness is defined by greater restrictions on action or a greater duty owed, not penalty size.
rationales.C: Incorrect. The comparison is between applicable laws and the Code, not a home-country default.

questions.stem: *Three principles under Standard I(A)* *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: (1) must comply with applicable laws or regulations related to their professional activities.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Three principles.
rationales.B:
Full statement: (1) must comply with applicable laws or regulations related to their professional activities; (2) must not engage in conduct that constitutes violation of Code and Standards, even though it may otherwise be legal; (3) in absence of any applicable law or regulation or when Code and Standards impose higher degree of responsibility than applicable laws and regulations, must adhere to Code and Standards.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates must adhere to the following principles: Members and candidates must comply with applicable laws or regulations related to their professional activities. Members and candidates must not engage in conduct that constitutes a violation of the Code and Standards, even though it may otherwise be legal. In the absence of any applicable law or regulation or when the Code and Standards impose a higher degree of responsibility than applicable laws and regulations, members and candidates must adhere to the Code and Standards.'
rationales.C: 3 principles.

questions.stem: *Intermediate steps for dissociation* under Standard I(A) *most likely*:

options.A: The only recognized method of dissociating from another's ethical violation is immediate resignation from the firm, without any prior escalation.
options.B: No intermediate steps are required; a member who personally abstains from the questionable conduct while remaining silent has fully dissociated.
options.C: may take following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with person committing violation are unsuccessful: FIRST step is to attempt to stop behavior by bringing it to attention of employer through supervisor or firm's compliance department.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Escalation to supervisors or compliance comes first; leaving the employer is a last resort.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Inaction can constitute participation; steps include removing oneself from the engagement and escalating.
rationales.C:
Full statement: may take following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with person committing violation are unsuccessful: FIRST step is to attempt to stop behavior by bringing it to attention of employer through supervisor or firm's compliance department; if this attempt unsuccessful, then must step away and dissociate from activity; dissociation practices differ based on member's/candidate's professional role — may include removing one's name from written reports or recommendations, asking for different assignment, or refusing to accept new client or continue to advise current client; INACTION combined with continuing association with those involved in illegal or unethical conduct may be construed as participation or assistance in illegal or unethical conduct.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates may take the following intermediate steps to dissociate from ethical violations of others when direct discussions with the person or persons committing the violation are unsuccessful. The first step is to attempt to stop the behavior by bringing it to the attention of the employer through a supervisor or the firm's compliance department. If this attempt is unsuccessful, then members and candidates have a responsibility to step away and dissociate from the activity. Dissociation practices will differ on the basis of the member's or candidate's professional role or responsibilities. Dissociation may include removing one's name from written reports or recommendations, asking for a different assignment, or refusing to accept a new client or continue to advise a current client. Inaction combined with continuing association with those involved in illegal or unethical conduct may be construed as participation or assistance in the illegal or unethical conduct.'

questions.stem: *Whistleblowing under Code and Standards* *most likely*:

options.A: CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates.
options.B: Members are required to report all suspected violations by peers to CFA Institute.
options.C: Reporting peers is prohibited as a breach of loyalty to colleagues.

rationales.A:
Full statement: CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates; although failure to report is less likely to be construed as violation than failure to dissociate from unethical conduct, impact of inactivity on integrity of capital markets can be significant; Code and Standards do NOT compel members and candidates to report violations to their governmental or regulatory organizations UNLESS such disclosure is mandatory under applicable law (voluntary reporting is often referred to as 'whistle-blowing'); such disclosure may be prudent under certain circumstances; should consult legal and compliance advisers for guidance.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'CFA Institute strongly encourages members and candidates to report potential violations of the Code and Standards committed by fellow members and candidates. Although a failure to report is less likely to be construed as a violation than a failure to dissociate from unethical conduct, the impact of inactivity on the integrity of capital markets can be significant. Although the Code and Standards do not compel members and candidates to report violations to their governmental or regulatory organizations unless such disclosure is mandatory under applicable law (voluntary reporting is often referred to as "whistle-blowing"), such disclosure may be prudent under certain circumstances. Members and candidates should consult their legal and compliance advisers for guidance.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reporting is STRONGLY ENCOURAGED, not required (absent a legal mandate).
rationales.C: Incorrect. Loyalty to colleagues never bars reporting potential Code violations.

questions.stem: *Cross-border investment products* Standard I(A) compliance *most likely*:

options.A: Product creators need only consider the law of the jurisdiction where the product is domiciled.
options.B: members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or products must be mindful of where these services/products will be sold and their places of origination.
options.C: Distribution-country regulations are the exclusive concern of local selling agents.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Creators and maintainers must be mindful of where products will be SOLD as well as originated.
rationales.B:
