Full statement: members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or products must be mindful of where these services/products will be sold and their places of origination; those responsible for providing services or creating/providing investment products must understand and comply with applicable laws and regulations in ALL relevant jurisdictions; must undertake necessary due diligence when transacting cross-border business to understand multiple applicable laws and regulations; should seek appropriate guidance including firm's compliance/legal departments and legal counsel outside organization to gain reasonable understanding of their responsibilities.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Guidance: 'Members and candidates involved in creating or maintaining investment services or investment products must be mindful of where these services or products will be sold and their places of origination. Those members or candidates who are responsible for providing the services or creating or providing investment products must understand and comply with applicable laws and regulations in all relevant jurisdictions. Members and candidates must undertake the necessary due diligence when transacting cross-border business to understand the multiple applicable laws and regulations. Given the complexity that can arise with business transactions in global markets, there may be some uncertainty surrounding which laws or regulations are considered applicable when activities are being conducted in multiple jurisdictions. Members and candidates should seek the appropriate guidance...'
rationales.C: Incorrect. The member's own compliance duty extends to the places of distribution.

questions.stem: *Documenting when dissociating* *most likely*:

options.A: Verbal notification to a colleague is sufficient evidence of dissociation.
options.B: Documentation is discouraged because it may create legal liability for the member.
options.C: when dissociating from an activity that violates Code and Standards, members and candidates should DOCUMENT the violation.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance says to DOCUMENT the violation and urge the firm to stop the conduct.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Documentation is a recommended protective step, not a liability to avoid.
rationales.C:
Full statement: when dissociating from an activity that violates Code and Standards, members and candidates should DOCUMENT the violation; this creates record supporting their position that they took appropriate steps to separate themselves from unethical activity.

Correct. Per Standard I(A) Compliance Practices: 'Dissociate from violations: When dissociating from an activity that violates the Code and Standards, members and candidates should document the violation.'

questions.stem: *Restricted lists* under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: if firm is unwilling to permit dissemination of adverse opinions about corporate client, members and candidates should encourage firm to remove the controversial company from its research universe and put it on a restricted list so that firm disseminates only factual information about company.
options.B: If a firm won't allow adverse opinions, analysts should publish favorable reports to preserve the client relationship.
options.C: Restricted lists are prohibited because they deprive clients of research coverage.

rationales.A:
Full statement: if firm is unwilling to permit dissemination of adverse opinions about corporate client, members and candidates should encourage firm to remove the controversial company from its research universe and put it on a restricted list so that firm disseminates only factual information about company; restricted lists help manage conflicts inherent in investment banking relationships.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Compliance Practices and Example 3 discussion: restricted lists are a recognized tool to eliminate research/investment-banking conflicts. Companies subject to potential offerings can be placed on restricted list to eliminate research pressure.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Publishing unwarranted favorable research violates independence and objectivity.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Moving the company to a restricted list and distributing only factual information is the recommended remedy.

questions.stem: *Special cost arrangements* under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: Accepting issuer-paid charter flights is best practice whenever it saves the member's firm money.
options.B: when attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to REJECT any special cost arrangements — should pay for their commercial transportation and hotel charges — because acceptance of such arrangements may create appearance of conflict.
options.C: Members must decline to attend issuer-sponsored events altogether.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Best practice is to REJECT special cost arrangements and pay for one's own commercial transport and lodging.
rationales.B:
Full statement: when attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to REJECT any special cost arrangements — should pay for their commercial transportation and hotel charges — because acceptance of such arrangements may create appearance of conflict; corporations should not reimburse members and candidates for air transportation.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Compliance Practices: 'Restrict special cost arrangements: When attending analyst events, best practice for members and candidates is to reject any special cost arrangements... Corporations should not reimburse members and candidates for air transportation.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Attendance is acceptable; the guidance targets the cost arrangements, not the event.

questions.stem: *Limit gifts under I(B)* *most likely*:

options.A: Absent an employer policy, there is no limit on the value of gifts a member may accept.
options.B: Token gifts are also prohibited unless pre-approved in writing by compliance.
options.C: even if member's/candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, must limit acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to TOKEN items.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Even without a firm policy, acceptance must be limited to TOKEN items.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Token items are permissible; the limit, not a blanket approval regime, is the guidance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: even if member's/candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, must limit acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to TOKEN items; Standard I(B) does not preclude customary, ordinary business-related entertainment as long as its purpose is not to influence or reward members or candidates.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Compliance Practices: 'Limit gifts: Even if a member's or candidate's employer does not have a policy on accepting gifts, members and candidates must limit the acceptance of gratuities and/or gifts to token items. Standard I(B) does not preclude customary, ordinary business-related entertainment as long as its purpose is not to influence or reward members or candidates.'

questions.stem: *Client gifts disclosure* under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: prior to accepting 'bonuses' or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients.
options.B: Gifts from clients need never be disclosed because clients are not third parties.
options.C: Gifts from clients must always be rejected outright under Standard I(B).

rationales.A:
Full statement: prior to accepting 'bonuses' or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients; if notification not possible prior to acceptance, must disclose to employer benefits previously accepted from clients; disclosure allows employer to make independent determination about extent to which gift may affect member's or candidate's independence and objectivity.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Guidance: 'Prior to accepting "bonuses" or gifts from clients, members and candidates should disclose to their employers such benefits offered by clients. If notification is not possible prior to acceptance, members and candidates must disclose to their employer benefits previously accepted from clients. Disclosure allows the employer to make an independent determination about the extent to which the gift may affect the member's or candidate's independence and objectivity.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Client gifts should be disclosed to the employer — best practice, before acceptance — so conflicts can be assessed.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Client-gratitude gifts differ from third-party inducements; acceptance with disclosure can be permissible.

questions.stem: *Firewalls in credit rating agencies* under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: not needed.
options.B: work of credit rating agencies raises concerns similar to those inherent in investment banking relationships.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Firewalls + independent validation.
rationales.B:
Full statement: work of credit rating agencies raises concerns similar to those inherent in investment banking relationships; analysts may face pressure to issue ratings at specific level because of other services agency offers companies (advising on development of structured products); rating agencies need to develop necessary FIREWALLS and protections to allow independent operations of their different business lines; when using information provided by credit rating agencies, members and candidates should be mindful of potential conflicts of interest and may need to INDEPENDENTLY VALIDATE the rating granted.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Guidance on Credit Rating Agency Opinions: 'Analysts may face pressure to issue ratings at a specific level because of other services the agency offers companies — namely, advising on the development of structured products. The rating agencies need to develop the necessary firewalls and protections to allow the independent operations of their different business lines. When using information provided by credit rating agencies, members and candidates should be mindful of the potential conflicts of interest. And because of the potential conflicts, members and candidates may need to independently validate the rating granted.'
rationales.C: Firewalls.

questions.stem: *Investment banking / research analyst collaboration* under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: always OK.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate ONLY when conflicts are adequately and effectively managed.

rationales.A: Only with adequate controls.
rationales.B: Managed collaboration.
rationales.C:
Full statement: having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate ONLY when conflicts are adequately and effectively managed; any such conflict that is not avoided must be disclosed (see Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts); requires firms to carefully balance conflicts of interest inherent in collaboration.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Guidance on Investment Banking Relationships: 'Although collaboration between research analysts and investment banking colleagues may benefit the firm and enhance market efficiency... it requires firms to carefully balance the conflicts of interest inherent in the collaboration. Having analysts work with investment bankers is appropriate only when the conflicts are adequately and effectively managed. Any such conflict that is not avoided must be disclosed; see Standard VI(A) Avoid or Disclose Conflicts.'

questions.stem: *Manager selection influence* under I(B) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates may find themselves on either side of manager selection process.
options.B: no concern.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates may find themselves on either side of manager selection process; can be involved in selecting external managers to receive portions of firm's or client's assets to manage OR represent their firms in trying to be selected to manage external client's assets; both sides of process must maintain their independence and objectivity to ensure decisions are made in best interest of firm's or client's assets; must not attempt to compromise objectivity of person making selection through offering gifts, benefits, or other considerations.

Correct. Per Standard I(B) Guidance and Example 11 (Mandel/Gomez violation): Standard I(B) applies both to gift receivers and gift givers. In manager selection processes, both sides must maintain independence and objectivity to ensure decisions are made in the best interests of the firm's or client's assets.
rationales.B: Both sides must be objective.
rationales.C: I(B) both sides.

questions.stem: *Monitoring online content* under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: A one-time compliance review at the moment of posting is sufficient for online content.
options.B: members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information.
options.C: Once published, online materials become the platform's responsibility, not the member's.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Members should REGULARLY monitor posted materials to ensure they remain current.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information; members and candidates who publish content through websites should also ensure that all reasonable precautions have been taken to protect website's integrity, confidentiality, and security and that website does not misrepresent any information and provides full disclosure.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Compliance Practices: 'Members and candidates should regularly monitor materials and content posted online or through social media to ensure that they contain current information. Members and candidates who publish content through websites should also ensure that all reasonable precautions have been taken to protect the website's integrity, confidentiality, and security and that the website does not misrepresent any information and provides full disclosure.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Responsibility for the accuracy and currency of one's professional content stays with the member.

questions.stem: *Avoiding plagiarism steps* under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: The only required step is keeping copies of one's own final published reports.
options.B: Attributing quotations to 'leading analysts and investment experts' generically satisfies attribution requirements.
options.C: to avoid plagiarism in preparing research reports/conclusions of analysis, should: (1) maintain copies — keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials relied on in preparing research report.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance lists several steps: maintain copies of source materials, attribute quotations and summaries to their sources, and attribute paraphrases.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Generic attribution without naming the source is itself an improper practice under the plagiarism guidance.
rationales.C:
Full statement: to avoid plagiarism in preparing research reports/conclusions of analysis, should: (1) maintain copies — keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials relied on in preparing research report; (2) attribute quotations — attribute to sources any direct quotations including projections, tables, statistics, model/product ideas, and new methodologies prepared by persons other than recognized financial and statistical reporting services or similar sources; (3) attribute summaries — attribute to sources any paraphrases or summaries of material prepared by others.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Avoiding Plagiarism: 'Maintain copies: Keep copies of all research reports, articles containing research ideas, material with new statistical methodologies, and other materials that were relied on in preparing the research report. Attribute quotations: Attribute to their sources any direct quotations, including projections, tables, statistics, model/product ideas, and new methodologies prepared by persons other than recognized financial and statistical reporting services or similar sources. Attribute summaries: Attribute to their sources any paraphrases or summaries of material prepared by others.'

questions.stem: *Description of qualifications and services* practice under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member understands limit of firm's or individual's capabilities and need to be accurate and complete in presentations.
options.B: Firms should let each employee draft personal marketing descriptions without central review.
options.C: Written summaries of firm qualifications are discouraged because they can quickly become outdated.

rationales.A:
Full statement: members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member understands limit of firm's or individual's capabilities and need to be accurate and complete in presentations; whether or not employer provides guidance, members and candidates must make certain they understand services firm performs and its qualifications and disclose that information without misrepresentation; each member/candidate should prepare summary of his or her own qualifications and experience and list of services member/candidate is capable of performing.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Compliance Practices — Description of Qualifications and Services: 'Members and candidates can prevent unintentional misrepresentations of the qualifications or services they or their firms provide if each member and candidate understands the limit of the firm's or individual's capabilities and the need to be accurate and complete in presentations. Whether or not their employer provides guidance, members and candidates must make certain that they understand the services the firm performs and its qualifications and disclose that information without misrepresentation. Each member and candidate should prepare a summary of his or her own qualifications and experience and a list of the services the member or candidate is capable of performing.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Understanding — and working from — the firm's prepared description of its qualifications and services prevents unintentional misrepresentation.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Maintained written descriptions are the recommended practice; staleness is managed by keeping them current.

questions.stem: *Third-party information incorporation* under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: no responsibility.
options.B: members and candidates must exercise care and diligence when incorporating third-party information into their own work.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Third-party accountability.
rationales.B:
Full statement: members and candidates must exercise care and diligence when incorporating third-party information into their own work; misrepresentations that result from using materials produced by outside parties BECOME THE RESPONSIBILITY of members and candidates when they incorporate that material into or make it part of their work; when providing information to clients from third parties, share responsibility for accuracy of marketing and distribution materials pertaining to third party's capabilities, services, and products; must disclose their intended use of external third parties and must not represent the work of others as their own.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Guidance on Use of Third-Party Information: 'Members and candidates must exercise care and diligence when incorporating third-party information into their own work. Misrepresentations that result from using materials produced by outside parties become the responsibility of members and candidates when they incorporate that material into or make it part of their work. When providing information to clients from third parties, members and candidates share a responsibility for the accuracy of the marketing and distribution materials that pertain to the third party's capabilities, services, and products. Members and candidates must disclose their intended use of external third parties and must not represent the work of others as their own.'
rationales.C: Third-party.

questions.stem: *Performance benchmark selection* under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: no framework.
options.B: no framework.
options.C: performance benchmark selection process is another area where misrepresentations may occur.

rationales.A: Comparable benchmark.
rationales.B: Benchmark selection.
rationales.C:
Full statement: performance benchmark selection process is another area where misrepresentations may occur; members and candidates may misrepresent success of their performance record through presenting benchmarks that are not comparable to their strategies; clients can be misled if benchmark's results are not reported on basis comparable to that of the fund's or client's results; best practice is selecting the most appropriate available benchmark from universe of available options; transparent presentation of appropriate performance benchmarks is important in providing clients with information useful for investment decisions; Standard I(C) does not require that a benchmark always be provided in order to comply; some investment strategies may not lend themselves to displaying an appropriate benchmark because of complexity or diversity of investments; when reference indexes such as 'cash plus' basis are used for hedge fund, must disclose reasons behind use of this reference index.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Guidance on Investment Performance: 'The performance benchmark selection process is another area where misrepresentations may occur. Members and candidates may misrepresent the success of their performance record through presenting benchmarks that are not comparable to their strategies. Further, clients can be misled if the benchmark's results are not reported on a basis comparable to that of the fund's or client's results. Best practice is selecting the most appropriate available benchmark from a universe of available options... However, Standard I(C) does not require that a benchmark always be provided in order to comply. Some investment strategies may not lend themselves to displaying an appropriate benchmark because of the complexity or diversity of the investments included.'

questions.stem: *Valuation shopping* under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: reporting misrepresentations may occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source.
options.B: Members may select whichever external valuation is highest for client reporting, provided the source is reputable.
options.C: Illiquid securities are exempt from misrepresentation concerns because their prices are inherently uncertain.

rationales.A:
Full statement: reporting misrepresentations may occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source; when different options are available, members and candidates may be TEMPTED to switch providers to obtain higher security valuations; process of shopping for values may misrepresent security's worth, lead to misinformed decisions to sell or hold investment, and result in overcharging clients for advisory fees; must take reasonable steps to provide accurate and reliable security pricing information to clients on consistent basis; changing pricing providers must NOT be based solely on justification that new provider reports higher current value of security; consistency in valuation process will improve value of security pricing information.

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Guidance: 'Reporting misrepresentations may also occur when valuations for illiquid or non-traded securities are available from more than one source. When different options are available, members and candidates may be tempted to switch providers to obtain higher security valuations. The process of shopping for values may misrepresent a security's worth... Members and candidates must take reasonable steps to provide accurate and reliable security pricing information to clients on a consistent basis. Changing pricing providers must not be based solely on the justification that the new provider reports a higher current value of a security. Consistency in the valuation process will improve the value of the security pricing information.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Shopping among sources for the most favorable value misrepresents performance; selection must be consistent and justified.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Valuation uncertainty heightens, rather than removes, the misrepresentation risk the guidance addresses.

questions.stem: *Omissions and cherry-picking* under I(C) *most likely*:

options.A: acceptable.
options.B: omission of fact or outcome can be misleading, especially in use of models and technical analysis processes.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: No cherry-picking.
rationales.B:
Full statement: omission of fact or outcome can be misleading, especially in use of models and technical analysis processes; when inputs are knowingly omitted, resulting outcomes may provide misleading information; additionally, outcomes from models must NOT be presented as fact because they represent expected results based on inputs and analysis process; omissions in performance measurement and attribution process can also misrepresent manager's performance and skill; members and candidates must NOT misrepresent performance history by engaging in practices that distort past investment performance (e.g., cherry-picking highest-performing accounts and presenting them as representative of a strategy).

Correct. Per Standard I(C) Guidance on Omissions: 'The omission of a fact or outcome can be misleading, especially in the use of models and technical analysis processes... When inputs are knowingly omitted, the resulting outcomes may provide misleading information to those who rely on information for making investment decisions. Additionally, the outcomes from models must not be presented as fact, because they represent the expected results based on the inputs and analysis process incorporated. Omissions in the performance measurement and attribution process can also misrepresent a manager's performance and skill. Members and candidates must not misrepresent performance history by engaging in practices that distort past investment performance (e.g., cherry-picking the highest-performing accounts and presenting them as representative of a strategy).'
rationales.C: No cherry-picking.

questions.stem: *Firm-level anti-misconduct policy* under I(D) *most likely*:

options.A: The guidance limits each member's obligation to policing their own individual behavior; encouraging the firm to adopt codes, policies, or procedures against misconduct falls outside Standard I(D).
options.B: Responsibility for preventing general misconduct rests entirely with the firm's compliance function, so individual members and candidates bear no role in deterring dishonesty at the organizational level.
options.C: in addition to ensuring that their own behavior is consistent with Standard I(D), to prevent general misconduct, members and candidates should encourage their firms to adopt a code of ethics to which every employee must subscribe and to make clear that any personal behavior that reflects poorly on the individual involved, the institution as a whole, or the investment industry will not be tolerated.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Guidance says members should ENCOURAGE their firms to adopt codes and policies deterring misconduct.
rationales.B: Incorrect. Individual conduct obligations and firm-level encouragement operate together.
rationales.C: Correct. Per Standard I(D) Compliance Practices: 'In addition to ensuring that their own behavior is consistent with Standard I(D), to prevent general misconduct, members and candidates should encourage their firms to adopt a code of ethics to which every employee must subscribe and to make clear that any personal behavior that reflects poorly on the individual involved, the institution as a whole, or the investment industry will not be tolerated.'

questions.stem: *Definition of 'professional activities' under I(D)* *most likely*:

options.A: professional activity is any activity or conduct that relates to financial analysis, investment management, security analysis, stewardship, or other similar professional endeavors AND either (1) involves activity or conduct in the workplace or academia or participation in the investment profession or security markets OR (2) explicitly or implicitly encompasses the use of the CFA charter or CIPM designation, membership in CFA Institute or CFA Institute local societies, or candidacy for a designation sponsored by CFA Institute.
options.B: A professional activity is defined narrowly as any task performed for compensation at the member's current employer, so unpaid engagements and conduct outside the employment relationship are excluded from Standard I(D).
options.C: The definition of professional activity is confined to direct portfolio management for clients; related endeavors such as security analysis, stewardship activities, and academic participation fall outside the standard's reach.

rationales.A: Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'A professional activity is any activity or conduct that relates to financial analysis, investment management, security analysis, stewardship, or other similar professional endeavors and either (1) involves activity or conduct in the workplace or academia or participation in the investment profession or security markets or (2) explicitly or implicitly encompasses the use of the CFA charter or CIPM designation, membership in CFA Institute or CFA Institute local societies, or candidacy for a designation sponsored by CFA Institute.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The definition turns on the nature of the activity — financial analysis, investment management, and similar — not on employment or pay.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Stewardship and similar professional activities are expressly within the definition, and academia is cited in the examples.

questions.stem: *Examples of professional activities* under I(D) *most likely*:

options.A: Interactions with colleagues outside the office are always personal conduct beyond the standard's reach.
options.B: include (but not limited to): interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in workplace or academia or when participating in investment profession.
options.C: Only client-facing interactions qualify as professional activities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Interactions with colleagues and employees when participating in the profession are included, wherever they occur.
rationales.B:
Full statement: include (but not limited to): interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in workplace or academia or when participating in investment profession; activities in which member/candidate represents to others he/she is a CFA charterholder, candidate, or CFA Institute member; conduct while working as unpaid officer, representative, or volunteer with CFA Institute or local society; conduct while attending or participating in CFA Institute, local society, or industry-related meeting, course, conference, or event; writing about investments or markets in articles, books, reports, message boards, or blogs outside workplace or academia; communicating with government agency, regulator, or other professional organization regarding information within scope of member's/candidate's professional responsibilities related to investment profession.

Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'Examples of conduct by members and candidates that are considered professional activities under this standard include — but are not limited to — the following: Interactions with colleagues, employees, or clients in the workplace or academia or when participating in the investment profession; Activities in which a member or candidate represents to others that he or she is a CFA charterholder, a candidate for a designation sponsored by CFA Institute, or a CFA Institute member; Conduct while working as an unpaid officer, representative, or volunteer with CFA Institute or a local society; Conduct while attending or participating in a CFA Institute, local society, or industry-related meeting, course, conference, or event; Writing about investments or the markets in articles, books, reports, message boards, or blogs outside the workplace or academia; Communicating with a government agency, regulator, or other professional organization regarding information within the scope of the member's or candidate's professional responsibilities related to the investment profession.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Colleague, employee, and academic interactions are all listed alongside client interactions.

questions.stem: *Personal bankruptcy under I(D)* *most likely*:

options.A: A personal bankruptcy filing is a per se violation of Standard I(D) because financial failure always reflects adversely on professional competence.
options.B: Because bankruptcy is a lawful legal remedy available to any debtor, its circumstances can never implicate the Code and Standards in any way.
options.C: personal bankruptcy may NOT reflect on integrity or trustworthiness of person declaring bankruptcy, but IF circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy MAY be a violation of this standard.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Bankruptcy alone may not reflect on integrity or trustworthiness.
rationales.B: Incorrect. If the circumstances involve fraudulent or deceitful conduct, the standard is implicated.
rationales.C:
Full statement: personal bankruptcy may NOT reflect on integrity or trustworthiness of person declaring bankruptcy, but IF circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy MAY be a violation of this standard; determination depends on facts and circumstances.

Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'Personal bankruptcy may not reflect on the integrity or trustworthiness of the person declaring bankruptcy, but if the circumstances of the bankruptcy involve fraudulent or deceitful business conduct, the bankruptcy may be a violation of this standard.'

questions.stem: *Misuse of Professional Conduct Program* *most likely*:

options.A: individuals may attempt to ABUSE CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as method of settling personal, political, or other disputes UNRELATED to professional ethics.
options.B: Filing a Professional Conduct complaint against another member is itself discouraged by CFA Institute.
options.C: The Code guarantees that enforcement action follows whenever any complaint is filed.

rationales.A:
Full statement: individuals may attempt to ABUSE CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as method of settling personal, political, or other disputes UNRELATED to professional ethics; CFA Institute is aware of this issue, and appropriate disciplinary policies, procedures, and enforcement mechanisms are in place to address misuse of the Code and Standards and the Professional Conduct Program in this way.

Correct. Per Standard I(D) Guidance: 'Individuals may attempt to abuse the CFA Institute Professional Conduct Program by actively seeking CFA Institute enforcement of the Code and Standards and, in particular, Standard I(D) as a method of settling personal, political, or other disputes unrelated to professional ethics. CFA Institute is aware of this issue, and appropriate disciplinary policies, procedures, and enforcement mechanisms are in place to address misuse of the Code and Standards and the Professional Conduct Program in this way.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. Reporting is encouraged; the guidance warns against BAD-FAITH use of the program to settle personal disputes.
rationales.C: Incorrect. Complaints trigger review, not guaranteed enforcement; abusive complaints are themselves misconduct.

questions.stem: *Achieving/maintaining competence activities* under I(E) *most likely*:

options.A: Competence may be maintained only through formal university coursework.
options.B: to achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider following activities: regularly engaging in professional development or continuing education program.
options.C: Reading industry publications is expressly excluded as insufficiently rigorous.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Multiple pathways qualify — professional development programs, self-study, conferences, credentials, and more.
rationales.B:
Full statement: to achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider following activities: regularly engaging in professional development or continuing education program; studying for or earning professional certifications or designations; attending conferences, seminars, or webinars; regularly participating in training offered by their employer; diligently engaging in informal continuing education or self-study (outside reading of subject matter articles, treatises, publications); participating in expert groups or organizations; becoming proficient with any new skill or knowledge, as necessary, when their professional responsibilities change.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Compliance Practices: 'To achieve and maintain competence, members and candidates should consider the following activities: Regularly engaging in a professional development or continuing education program; Studying for or earning professional certifications or designations; Attending conferences, seminars, or webinars; Regularly participating in training offered by their employer; Diligently engaging in informal continuing education or self-study, such as through outside reading of subject matter articles, treatises, and publications; Participating in expert groups or organizations; Becoming proficient with any new skill or knowledge, as necessary, when their professional responsibilities change.'
rationales.C: Incorrect. Staying current through industry reading is among the recognized activities.

questions.stem: *No mandated CE program under I(E)* *most likely*:

options.A: Standard I(E) mandates completion of CFA Institute's Professional Learning program each year.
options.B: Members must certify a fixed number of continuing-education hours annually to retain membership.
options.C: although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does NOT mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan.

rationales.A: Incorrect. No particular program or provider is mandated by the standard.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The standard contemplates ongoing development without prescribing hour counts or certification.
rationales.C:
Full statement: although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does NOT mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan; members and candidates can attain and maintain the competence needed to fulfill their professional responsibilities in a variety of ways.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Although Standard I(E) contemplates ongoing professional development to maintain proficiency and competence, this standard does not mandate participation in a particular continuing education program or professional development plan. Members and candidates can attain and maintain the competence needed to fulfill their professional responsibilities in a variety of ways.'

questions.stem: *Knowledge / skills / abilities definitions* under I(E) *most likely*:

options.A: knowledge = body of information applied directly to performance of function and how effectively it is applied.
options.B: Knowledge, skills, and abilities are synonymous terms used interchangeably in the guidance.
options.C: Skills refer to theoretical information, while knowledge refers to manual or applied techniques.

rationales.A:
Full statement: knowledge = body of information applied directly to performance of function and how effectively it is applied; skills = capabilities to perform role-specific act or function to complete specific tasks and achieve professional goals; abilities = capabilities and attitudes that support behaviors that result in observable outcomes.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Knowledge is the body of information applied directly to the performance of a function and how effectively it is applied. Skills are capabilities to perform a role-specific act or function to complete specific tasks and achieve professional goals. Abilities are capabilities and attitudes that support behaviors that result in observable outcomes.'
rationales.B: Incorrect. The three terms are distinctly defined in the guidance.
rationales.C: Incorrect. That reverses the definitions: knowledge is applied information; skills are developed proficiencies.

questions.stem: *Competence beyond education* under I(E) *most likely*:

options.A: education equals competence.
options.B: competence is NOT limited to examination of the education level of a member or candidate.
options.C: no framework.

rationales.A: Education ≠ competence.
rationales.B:
Full statement: competence is NOT limited to examination of the education level of a member or candidate; highly educated investment consultant may not have experience to undertake consulting activities in unfamiliar area of practice; accounting professional supremely competent in providing financial statement audits may not be competent in assessing compliance with performance presentation standards (such as GIPS standards); investment consultant hired to conduct search for investment manager focused on sustainable investing may not have skills needed to competently evaluate managers with that focus.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Competence is not limited to an examination of the education level of a member or candidate. A highly educated investment consultant may not have the experience to undertake consulting activities in an unfamiliar area of practice. An accounting professional supremely competent in providing financial statement audits may not be competent in assessing compliance with performance presentation standards, such as the GIPS standards. Or an investment consultant hired to conduct a search for an investment manager focused on sustainable investing may not have the skills needed to competently evaluate managers with that focus.'
rationales.C: Beyond education.

questions.stem: *Extending competence when responsibilities change* under I(E) *most likely*:

options.A: Competence acquired on entering the profession remains sufficient for a member's entire career.
options.B: When responsibilities expand, members should decline the new duties rather than build new competence.
options.C: over time, professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities.

rationales.A: Incorrect. Changing and expanding responsibilities require new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities over time.
rationales.B: Incorrect. The expected response is extending competence, not refusing growth in responsibility.
rationales.C:
Full statement: over time, professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities; members and candidates will develop and refine their skills and abilities as their careers progress; Standard I(E) imposes duty to not simply achieve but also MAINTAIN competence, emphasizing need for members to continuously maintain or improve competence required of their professional position.

Correct. Per Standard I(E) Guidance: 'Over time, the professional responsibilities of members and candidates may change or expand, requiring new or different knowledge, skills, and abilities. Members and candidates will develop and refine their skills and abilities as their careers progress. Standard I(E) imposes the duty to not simply achieve but also maintain competence, emphasizing the need for members to continuously maintain or improve the competence required of their professional position.'
